glossary.def: Any setting fixed before training that the optimizer is not allowed to change — learning rate, tree depth, regularization strength, dropout rate. Tuning is the search over these, scored by cross-validation, that produces the model you actually ship.

The set of allowed hyperparameter configurations, usually the Cartesian product of each knob's range. Its design — log vs linear scales, sensible bounds, and which knobs to include — quietly determines how easy or impossible the tuning problem is.

Exhaustive tuning: pick a finite set of values per hyperparameter, evaluate every point in the Cartesian product, keep the best. Simple and reproducible, but its cost is exponential in the number of hyperparameters and it wastes budget re-testing axes that do not move the score.

Sample each hyperparameter independently from a distribution for a fixed number of trials. At equal budget it matches or beats grid search when only a few hyperparameters matter, because it tests far more distinct values of the important axes (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012).

Informed, sequential tuning: fit a cheap probabilistic surrogate (often a Gaussian process) to past trials, then query where an acquisition function predicts the best trade-off between low predicted loss and high uncertainty. Sample-efficient when each evaluation is expensive.

The rule that turns a surrogate's posterior mean and uncertainty into a single score for choosing the next query, balancing exploitation (go where the mean is good) against exploration (go where uncertainty is high). Common choices are confidence bounds (mu - kappa*sigma) and Expected Improvement.

A tournament on budget: start many configurations with a tiny budget, cull the worst fraction, give survivors geometrically more budget, and repeat. Total compute per round stays roughly constant, concentrating spend on configurations that prove themselves early.

Runs successive halving across several brackets with different exploration/budget trade-offs, hedging against the unknown of how well early performance predicts final performance. The bracket idea (often as the asynchronous ASHA variant) is the default scheduler in modern tuners.

refs.cite: Bergstra, J. & Bengio, Y. (2012). Random Search for Hyper-Parameter Optimization. JMLR 13.
Snoek, J., Larochelle, H. & Adams, R. P. (2012). Practical Bayesian Optimization of Machine Learning Algorithms. NeurIPS 2012.
Li, L., Jamieson, K., DeSalvo, G., Rostamizadeh, A. & Talwalkar, A. (2017). Hyperband: A Novel Bandit-Based Approach to Hyperparameter Optimization. JMLR 18.
Jamieson, K. & Talwalkar, A. (2016). Non-stochastic Best Arm Identification and Hyperparameter Optimization. AISTATS 2016.
Li, L. et al. (2020). A System for Massively Parallel Hyperparameter Tuning (ASHA). MLSys 2020.
Akiba, T., Sano, S., Yanase, T., Ohta, T. & Koyama, M. (2019). Optuna: A Next-generation Hyperparameter Optimization Framework. KDD 2019.
